observations.recommendation:
1.18 We recommended and Management agreed to:
1.18.1 Direct Municipal Health Officer and the Municipal Accountant to
have coordination with regards to the monetary value of the actual
physical accomplishments to be used as basis for liquidation funds
and the subsequent recognition of Subsidy from GOCCs;
1.18.2 Direct the Municipal Accountant to:
1.18.2.1 Revisit the provisions of COA Circular No. 2023-003 dated
June 14, 2023, regarding the accounting for
funds/capitation received from PhilHealth Inc., utilization
of funds, and liquidation of frontloaded funds of the E-
Konsulta program, ; and
1.18.2.2 Make representations to the PhilHealth Regional Office VI
to reconcile the balances per books of the municipality and
that of the regional office;
1.18.2.3 Based on the reconciled balances effect following correcting
entries:
Debit Credit
Due to NGAs Xxxx
Due to GOCCs- Xxxx
To reclassify to correct account the reconciled balance of E-
Konsulta.
Appropriate Asset/ Xxxx
Expense Account
Prior Period Xxxx
Adjustment
To recognize the asset acquired/ expense incurred.
Prior Period Xxxx
Adjustment
Subsidy from GOCCs Xxxx
To recognize the related Subsidy from GOCCs based on the
reconciled liquidated funds.
1.18.2.4 Comply with the correct accounts and accounting entries in
recording of transactions related to PhilHealth Konsulta
Program as prescribed in COA Circular No. 2023-003
dated June 14, 2023.

2.9 We recommended and Management agreed to:
2.9.1 Revisit the provision of COA Circular No. 2022-004 dated May 31,
2022, and COA Circular No. 2024-006 dated March 14, 2024, on the
proper classification and accounting treatment, records to be
maintained, and reports to be submitted pertaining to semi-
expendable properties; and
2.9.2 Direct the Accountant to:
2.9.2.1 Henceforth observe the correct recording and
classification of expenses pertaining to semi-expendable
properties and expenses;
2.9.2.2 Maintain appropriate Ledger Cards for each type of
semi-expendable property.

3.10 We recommend and Management agreed to require:
3.10.1 The Municipal Accountant, Budget Officer, and Disaster Risk
Reduction Management Officer to:
3.10.1.1 Reconcile the variance in the balances per NFS,
ledger/post-closing trial balance, and the bank balance by
determining the correct amount of unexpended LDRRMF
to be transferred to the Trust Liablities- DRRMF
account;
3.10.1.2 Make available for supplemental appropriation any
unexpended balances from CYs 2018 and earlier.
3.10.2 The Municipal Accountant to:
3.10.2.1 Remove from the composition of Trust Liabilities-
DRRMF account as disclosed in the NFS the unexpended
balance pertaining to CYs 2018 and/or earlier; and
3.10.2.2 Revise the Notes to Financial Statements to reflect the
necessary adjustments that may arise from the
reconciliation.

4.14 We recommended and Management agreed to direct the Municipal
Accountant to:
4.14.1 Record the following adjustment in the General Fund and Special
Education Fund to correct the net effect of errors in the Government
Equity account:
Account Account Name Debit Credit
Code
General Fund
3-01-01-010 Prior Period Adjustment 73,878.44
2-05-01-010 Deferred RPT 73,878.44
40
Special Education Fund
3-01-01-010 Prior Period Adjustment 73,878.44
2-05-01-020 Deferred SET 73,878.44
4.14.2 Regularly reconcile the Deferred Real Property Tax (RPT) and
Special Education Tax (SET) with actual collections to avoid
carryover errors; and
4.14.3 Strictly comply with the provisions of Sections 22 and 23 of NGAS
Manual for LGUs and Paragraph 27 of IPSAS No. 1.

We recommended and the
Management agreed to require the Municipal
Accountant to:
a. Review and adjust the recognition of
Depreciation Expense in all affected PPEs pursuant
to Item 71 of IPSAS 17 and COA Circular 2004-
003 dated October 4, 2004; and
b. Compute the Accumulated Depreciation with
consideration of the remaining life on the
serviceable asset based on the period that the asset
was in service from the date of
acquisition/completion to reflect the carrying
amount of the affected PPE at the end of CY
2023.

We recommended and the Management agreed to
require the Municipal Accountant, Municipal
Treasurer, and Municipal Budget Officer to:
2.8.1 Explain the basis for paying disbursements
and use of funds intended for a specific PPA for another
purpose as reflected in the cash deficit per the cash deficit per
SAOB;
2.8.2 Justify how the cash overdraft of ₱13.132
million in the GF be resolved so that funds
would be available to meet all obligations
and liabilities;
2.8.3 Require the Local Finance Committee:
2.8.3.1 To analyze the income and
expenditure patterns, and formulate and
recommend fiscal plans and policies for
consideration of the finance committee,
as provided in Article 122 (f.2.vi)
of the IRR of RA 7160, to ensure that
no cash overdraft in any government
funds shall be incurred at any given time of the
year;
2.8.3.2 To conduct a semi-annual review and
general examination of costs and
accomplishments against performance
standards applied in undertaking
development projects pursuant to
Item Article 412 (h) of the IRR of RA
7160;
2.8.4 Open separate depository bank
accounts for the 20% DF (to include CY
2022 20% DF balances as of
December 31, 2023 and the LDRRMF, to
ensure funds are used solely for these
purposes.

We recommended and the Inventory Committee agreed
to prepare and submit the Approved Physical Inventory
Plan, to the COA Audit Team at least ten (10) days
before inventory-taking activities pursuant to COA
Circular 2020-006, to ensure that all planned inventory
activities/procedures are efficiently and effectively
performed by the Committee members.

We recommended and the Management agreed to
require the Property Officer and Municipal Accountant:
4.7.1 Reconcile the RPCPPE and the PPELC of accounting
records to ascertain the accuracy of the recorded PPE and to
resolve the variance in the total amount of ₱85,538.10;
4.7.2 Report in the IIURP the unserviceable PPE
items to reflect the actual total unserviceable items;
4.7.3 Facilitate the disposal of 234 unserviceable PPE
items to relieve AOs of their accountabilities, to prevent further
deterioration of the equipment, to decongest the storage area of the
unserviceable property;
4.7.4 Update and tag the properties of the agency with their
corresponding PAR number and ensure the reconciliation of the
PAR number in the Property Sticker to the inventory records; and
4.7.5 Ensure that the end users of the PPE items have their copy
of the Property Acknowledgement Receipt.

We recommended and the Management agreed to
require the Municipal Engineer to:
5.6.1 Justify the excess of ₱1.068 million for
disbursements of materials and labor wages and project time
duration from five to 121 days over the approved cost/duration
per POW;
5.6.2 Prepare individual POWs or sub-POWs indicating technical
details for each barangay location where the PPA:
Portland Cement Concrete Pavement was implemented;
5.6.3 Submit the latest Statement of Work Accomplishment
(SWA) of the Construction of Balay Dalangpan (2023).

We recommended and the Management agreed to
require the Municipal Engineer to:
6.6.1 Justify why only one Program of Work/PERT CPM was
prepared for four (4) separate projects;
6.6.2 Explain the inconsistent costing and duration per work
item as reflected in the POW, PERT/CPM, and Statement of
Work Accomplishment;
6.6.3 Prepare Individual POW/ Sub POW for each Welcome Arch in
four different locations to ascertain the corresponding cost and
work duration per work item;
6.6.4 Justify the first partial payment of ₱2.156 million representing
the 69.62 percent accomplishment per SWA as of March 1, 2023,
while the accomplishment rate is not reflective of the first nor the
second revision of PERT/CPMs;
6.6.5 Submit the Contractor’s Request/Notices for the extension of
contract time and the evaluation / approval by the Management;
6.6.6 Justify the 2nd revision of the contract duration to start on
December 21, 2022, which is already beyond 7 days from the NTP
date, December 12, 2022.

We recommended and the Management agreed to require:
BAC and the BAC Secretariat to:
7.6.1 Include in the APP all APPs subject to procurement activities
regardless of goods or services of any mode of procurement. If an
APP is not indicated in the APP, prepare a revise or update the
APP for approval by the Municipal Mayor;
7.6.2 Submit the perfected Contracts and Purchase Order and
supporting documents within five days upon approval for evaluation
pursuant to Item 1.1 of COA Circular 2009-001, dated February 12,
2009;
7.6.3 For procurement through the alternative mode of procurement:
7.6.3.1 Post the RFQs in the PhilGEPS website, the website of the
procuring entity, if available, and at any conspicuous place of the
premises of the municipality;
7.3.6.2 Prepare the Abstract of Quotations setting forth the names of
those who responded to the RFQ, their corresponding price
quotations, and the lowest calculated quotation submitted;
7.3.6.3 Require the suppliers to submit the documents required in
Appendix A of Annex H, of the IRR of RA 9184, namely, Mayor’s
Permit, PhilGEPs Registration, Income/Business Tax Return, and
Omnibus Sworn Statement;
Property/Supply Officer and Municipal Engineer:
7.6.4 Require the Property/Supply Officer/any authorized
representative from the General Services Office to accept all deliveries
and to affix the signature in the AIR as proof of acceptance and in the
RIS for the issuance of the materials to end users;
7.6.5 Require the Municipal Engineer or any authorized
representative to inspect only the delivered materials and affix a
signature in the Inspected portion of the AIR as proof of the inspection
conducted and in the RIS for the request of the materials;
7.6.6 Require that Delivery Receipts be dated as to the actual date of
delivery of materials.

We recommended and the Management agreed to:
8.8.1 Strengthen monitoring activities and come-up with
realistic/ doable project implementation schedule;
8.8.2 Streamline planning and procurement activities of all
infrastructure related PPAs to fast track implementation of all
on-going/ not started PPAs;
8.8.3 Submit the status of implementation of PPAs cited in
Observation Number 2;
8.8.4 Refrain from programming generic PPAs, instead, consider
exact PPAs that are specific, doable, and to be supported with
Program of Works, the location is free from legal impediments and
ready for implementation; and
8.8.5 Submit copies of the Sangguniang Bayan (SB) approving the
implementation of PPAs totaling ₱3.812 million.

We recommended and the Management agreed to require:
Direct the Municipal Accountant to:
9.16.1 Transfer the amount of ₱324,589.80 to the Trust Fund by
crediting the account of the Trust Liability- DRRM;
9.16.2 Conduct regular verification and reconciliation of
unexpended balances of fund transfer;
Direct the LDRRMC to:
9.16.3 Update subsequently the CYs LDRRMP by formulating and
incorporating DRRM PPAs requiring the highest priority in terms of
urgency and impact on the four thematic areas of DRRM;
Direct the MDRRMO to:
9.16.4 Ensure the optimal utilization of the LDRRMF by monitoring
its utilization to effectively implement the planned PPAs within the
targeted schedule.

We recommended and the Management agreed to:
11.17.1 Justify the delay in the implementation of the contract by
166 days;
11.17.2 Strictly comply with the provisions on Contract Time
Extension, page 97 of the Manual of Procedures for the
Procurement of Infrastructure Projects;
11.17.3 Submit PAG-ASA Report on dates when requested contract
extensions were due to bad weather conditions or typhoons;
11.17.4 Justify the payment of the 5th partial billing representing only
62.60 percent accomplishment per SWA as of October 31, 2023,
despite the last approved extended contract completion being on
July 5, 2023;
11.17.5 Justify why Liquidated Damages were not imposed on the
progress payment dated December 7, 2023 after the extended
contract period on July 5, 2023, has already lapsed.

We recommended and the Management agreed to the
following:
12.20.1 Furnish the Auditor with a copy of perfected contracts
and purchase orders within five (5) working days upon approval
together with the supporting documents for review:
12.20.1.1 Certified copies of the invitation to observers and
evidence of receipt for the bidding process conducted to
procurement in Table I;
12.20.1.2 Legal, Financial, and Technical Requirements along
with contract documents to ascertain compliance with the
PQER;
12.21 Adhere to the posting requirements for Bid Documents,
Supplemental/Bid Bulletins, Notices of Award, and Notices to
Proceed in the LGU's PhilGEPS;
12.22 Justify why a warranty security in the form of retention
money or a special bank guarantee was not required.

We recommended and the Management agreed to:
13.12 Hence after, secure SB authorization, by a resolution or
ordinance, for the local chief executives to enter into contracts, save
for cases where the appropriation is not in generic terms (i.e., a
specific SB approval is no longer required).

We recommended and the Management agreed to:
14.11.1 Require the SDO to prepare a liquidation
voucher which shall be submitted with supporting
documents to the Accountant;
14.11.2 Require the Accountant to certify that previous cash
advances have been fully liquidated;
14.11.3 Ascertain that cash advances are actually liquidated
and that no funds remain with the DCSPGT at the end of the
feeding periods;
14.11.4 Ascertain that the CBs of DCSPGTs are reviewed and
certified by an appropriate personnel;
14.11.5 Observe the procurement process through Negotiated
Procurement-Community Participation and establish local
sources; and
14.11.6 Open a separate and distinct bank account for the SFP.

We recommended and the Management agreed to
require:
15.7.1 The Accountable Officer to submit the following:
15.7.1.1 Justification for non-submission of the Notice of
Loss, Request for Relief of Accountability, and related
documents per COA Memorandum No. 92-751, dated
February 24, 1992;
15.7.1.2 The Municipal Accountant to review the Depreciated
Replacement Cost of the four motor vehicles as of the date of
the traffic incident and compare with the GSIS insurance
coverage to ascertain the amount of loss of PPE and to set up the
corresponding receivables from the concerned accountable officer
to be collected as provided in Item 7.10 of COA Circular No.
2020-004 dated January 31, 2020;

Streamline planning and procurement activities of all infrastructure related PPAs to fast track project implementation of all on-going/not started PPAs;

Require the Municipal Development Council to:

b.1 Appraise and prioritize socioeconomic development programs and projects; and

b.2 Coordinate, monitor, and evaluate the implementation of development programs and projects.

Refrain from programming generic PPAs, instead, consider exact PPAs that are specific, doable and supported with Program of Works, the location is free from legal impediments and ready for implementation; and

Strengthen monitoring activities and come-up with realistic/doable project implementation schedule.

We recommended and the Management agreed to require the Municipal Accountant to:
Review and adjust the recognition of Depreciation Expense in all affected PPEs pursuant to Item 71 of IPSAS 17 and COA Circular 2004-003 dated October 4, 2004; and
Compute the Accumulated Depreciation with consideration of the remaining life on the serviceable asset based on the period that the asset was in service from the date of acquisition/completion to reflect the carrying amount of the affected PPE at the end of CY 2023.

We recommended and the Inventory Committee agreed to prepare and submit the Approved Physical Inventory Plan, to the COA Audit Team at least ten (10) days before inventory-taking activities pursuant to COA Circular 2020-006, to ensure that Committee Members will perform all planned inventory activities/procedures effectively and efficiently.

We recommended and the Management agreed to:
Henceforth, secure prior SB authorization, by a resolution or ordinance, for the local chief executives to enter into contracts, save for cases where the appropriation is not in generic terms (i.e., a specific SB approval is no longer required).

Require the recipient Barangays submit to the Municipal Accountant the following:

Approved Project Expenditures/Estimate of Expenses/Program of Works indicating project objective and expected output; 

Deed of Undertaking; and

Report of Checks Issued (RCI) and the Report of Disbursement (RD) on the utilization of the fund transferred.

We recommended and Management agreed to require the Municipal Budget Officer to submit the approved appropriation ordinance authorizing the utilization of the 20% DF for the implementation of the reprogrammed PPAs.

We recommended and Management agreed to require the LDRRMO to submit the Report on Sources and Utilization of DRRMF to the AT certified by the Municipal Accountant on or before the 15th day following the end of each month, pursuant to Section 5.1.5 of COA Circular No. 2012-002 dated September 12, 2012.

Discontinue the use of the LDRRMF for administrative and operating expenditures; and

Ensure that all expenditures from the LDRRMF comply with the allowable expenses as prescribed by DILG Memorandum Circular No. 2012-73 and DBM-DILG-CSC Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2014-1.

Require the Municipal Accountant:

Properly monitor the disbursements of Financial Assistance according to the purpose; and

Return unexpended balance of the Financial Assistance to the National Treasury.

Require the Municipal Treasurer to:

Strengthen the collection of RPT/SET thru information or post the notice of the dates when the tax may be paid without interest at a conspicuous and publicly accessible place at the city or municipal hall. Said notice shall likewise be published in a newspaper of general circulation in the locality once a week for two (2) consecutive weeks (Section 249 of the same RA); and

For delinquent accounts, send notices to delinquent taxpayer and cause the posting of the Notice of Delinquency pursuant to Section 254 of the same RA.

Monitor and assess collection performance not only on the Performance Targets provided by oversight agencies but also on the actual RPT/SET due and collectible for the current year. 

Direct the Municipal Assessor to:

Coordinate with the Provincial Assessor for the revised valuation and assessment of real properties in the municipality pursuant to Section 219 of RA 7160 for an adjustment in the RPT and SET.
We recommend and Management agreed to discontinue contracting the services of the private lawyers performing legal services for the concerns and issues of the municipality.
Instruct the Municipal Accountant and the Property Custodian to maintain the Propety, Plant and Equipment Ledger Cards (PPELC) and the Property Cards (PC), respectively, per category of PPE.

We recommended that management agreed to:


   a. Amend/adjust the RPCPPE/Accounting records for ascertained reconciling
      PPE items reported in the RPCPPE but not recorded  by  the  Accounting
      Unit or vice versa to resolve the variance  in  the  total  amount  of
      ₱2.440 million;


   b.  Report  separately  the  732  Semi-expendable  items  costing   below
      15,000.00 in the total amount of ₱1.926 million;

   c.  Prepare  reclassification  of  Semi-expendable  property  which  were
      recognized  as  PPE  to  the  affected   appropriate   semi-expendable
      inventory accounts                        (if not yet issued  to  end-
      user), expense accounts  (if  issued  within  the  year),           or
      accumulated surplus/(deficit)/retained earnings accounts (if issued in
                prior years) pursuant to Item 5.4.1 of COA Circular 2016-006
      dated             December 29, 2016;

   d. Record all PPE items including those found at station in the  Property
      Cards and Property Ledger Cards and to be maintained,  henceforth,  by
      the Property Unit and Accounting Unit, respectively;


   e. Take appropriate action for the 18 PPE items which are either  without
      cost/value/donated/not taken up as PPE, totaling ₱3.274million

      e.1 for PPE items  without  cost/value,  coordinate  with  the  source
         agency as to the status of said PPE items, if donated,


         e.1.1  secure Invoice and Receipt of  Property  and  other  related
              documents supporting the transfer of PPE; and
         e.1.2   ascertain cost/value based on the market/fair value  or  by
              appraisal.


      e.2 for Donated PPE items to  other  agency,  furnish  a  copy  of  an
         Authority from the Municipal Mayor for the donation and Invoice and
         Receipt of Property donated;


      e.3 for PPE items found at station, reported in the  RPCPPE,  and  not
         taken as PPE per accounting  record,  coordinate  with  the  source
         agency as to the status of said PPE items, if donated,


            e.3.1 Secure Invoice and Receipt of Property and  other  related
             documents supporting the transfer of PPE; and


            e.3.2  Prepare Journal Entry Voucher to take up the donated  PPE
             item.


   f. Amend Property Numbers tagged to PPE items corresponding  to  the  PPE
      sub-major account group and General Ledger  account  provided  in  the
      Revised Chart of Accounts prescribed under the Accounting  Manuals  of
      the respective Sectors (Item 5.6 of COA Cir. 2020-006, 1/31/20).


   g. Report the 7 unserviceable  PPE  and  semi-expendable  items  totaling
      ₱2.260  million  in  the   Inventory   and   Inspection   Report   for
      Unserviceable Property (IIRUP) and indicate the mode of disposal;


   h. Invite the COA Auditor/Representative to witness the succeeding
      physical inventory taking in CY 2022 and years thereafter.

We recommended that management agreed to:

1. Require the collectors to  turn  over/remit  all  collections  daily  and
   intact to the Liquidating Officer in compliance to Sections 26 and 31  of
   COA Circular No. 92-382;


2. Closely supervise and monitor the daily collections  and  remittances  of
   Collecting Officers;

3. Refrain from issuing new and unused pad  of  ORs  unless  the  previously
   issued and used ORs and corresponding collections  are  remitted  to  the
   Liquidating Officer;

4. Direct the Municipal Treasurer  to  exercise  closer  monitoring  on  all
   collectors; and

5. Require the Municipal Accountant to prepare adjusting entries for the
   collections pertaining to CY 2021 recognized as income in January 2022
   and adjust to PYA as necessary.

We recommended that management agreed to:


   a. Direct the MPDO and other concerned departments to formulate  specific
      project/activity, indicating the brief description, location, time  of
      implementation, for every program  that  partakes  the  nature  of  an
      investment and capital expenditures;


   b.  Coordinate  with  the   MLDC   in   the   formulation   of   specific
      project/activity for inclusion in the LDP and AIP;

   c. Ensure that the 20% DF is optimally  utilized  and  only  for  capital
      expenditures to help achieve the desirable socio-economic  development
      and environmental outcomes of the LGU pursuant to Item 2.3 of DILG DBM
      Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2017-1 dated February 22, 2017;

   d. Henceforth, require the  approval  or  authorization  from  the  Local
      Sanggunian     prior      to      implementation      of      specific
      programs/projects/activities funded  out  of  lump  sum  appropriation
      under the 20% DF pursuant to Article 4 (d) of the Rules and Regulation
      Implementing the Local Government Code (LGC); and

   e. Cause the revision of the AIP or be updated for approval by the SB.

   Management Comment


   The management commented that the PPAs for 20% DF, specific projects  and
   activities were already determined during the assembly  and  consultation
   among the members of the Local Development Council (LDC). The  PPAs  were
   assessed in terms of being doable, completeness of  supporting  documents
   like plan or program of works, location, cost involved and  relevance  to
   the needs of the constituents and recipients.


   It was only in the prescribed Annual Investment Program format  that  the
   PPA appeared as generic or general in nature.


   In view of this, the Management assured that  the  details  of  the  PPAs
   listed in the AIP are always ready to the Audit Team  whenever  requested
   for reference and evaluation.


   As to  the  maintenance  of  heavy  equipment  charged  to  20%  DF,  the
   management pointed out that the equipment was used in the  implementation
   of PPAs charged to the fund. But they assured that  allocations  for  the
   maintenance of heavy equipment be made available in the general services.

   Auditor’s Rejoinder


   We appreciate the undertaking in the identification of PPAs for  the  20%
   Development Fund in cooperation and proper coordination of  the  LDC  and
   with other stakeholders in project planning. However,  we  recommend  the
   revision of the AIP or its updating for approval by the SB the  inclusion
   of  specific  PPAs  as  identified  by  the  LDC  to  include  the  brief
   description of all PPAs in the AIP for reference and guidance in audit.

We recommended that management agreed to:


   1. Cause the submission by  the  recipient  Barangays  to  the  Municipal
      Accountant  (MA) of the following:


     a. Approved Project Expenditures/Estimate of Expenses/Program of Works
        indicating project objective and expected output;


     b. Deed of Undertaking; and


     c. Report of Checks Issued (RCI) and the Report of  Disbursement  (RD)
        on the utilization of the  fund  transferred  by  the  implementing
        barangay; and

   2. Direct the MA to use the  account  Due  from  LGUs  on  the  grant  of
      financial assistance to barangays.

We recommended that management agreed to:


   1. Direct the LDRRMO to coordinate closely with the  Municipal  DRRMC  in
      the formulation of a  complete  and  integrated  LDRRMP  to  formulate
      detailed programs, projects and activities and to follow the timetable
      in the implementation of programmed projects/activities; and


   2. Local Sanggunian to approve only appropriation for  PPAs  specifically
      defined in a manner that the brief description, location, appropriated
      amount, time of implementation are described for  easy  implementation
      reference and guidance.

We recommended that management agreed to:


   1. Require the Municipal Budget Officer (MBO) to justify the  charges  of
      PPE totaling ₱229,200.00 to MOOE expense account as  reported  in  the
      Obligation Request; and


   2. Direct the MBO to properly classify the  erroneous  charges  to  other
      appropriate budgetary accounts.

A. Require the Municipal Accountant:

     a. to record procured inventory items to their  appropriate  inventory
        accounts;


     b. to maintain and post in  their  respective  Supplies  Ledger  Cards
        inventory items procured; and


     c. to reconcile with the inventory of welfare goods  and  supplies  to
        ascertain discrepancies, if any.


   B. Require the Supply Officer:


     a. to maintain and post in the Stock Cards the receipt  and  issuances
        of welfare goods and supplies based on the approved RIS issued;


     b. to prepare the Report of Supplies and Materials Issued  (RSMI)  and
        submit the same to the  Accounting  for  the  recording  of  issued
        welfare goods and supplies supported with the RIS and  Distribution
        List of welfare goods given to beneficiaries;

     c. to take an inventory of welfare goods and supplies and reconcile
        with the Stock Cards to determine discrepancies, if any.

A. Require that, henceforth, the Municipal Accountant:

     a. to record vaccines to their appropriate inventory accounts;


     A. to maintain and post to their respective Supplies Ledger Cards  the
        donated vaccines received; and


     B. to reconcile with the inventory of vaccines received and on-hand to
        ascertain discrepancies, if any.


   C. Require that, henceforth, the Supply Officer:


     a. to maintain and post in the Stock Cards the receipt  and  issuances
        of donated vaccines received;


     b. to prepare the Report of Supplies and Materials Issued  (RSMI)  and
        submit the same to the  Accounting  for  the  recording  of  issued
        vaccines supported with the RIS and List of Vaccinated Individuals;

     c. to take an inventory of vaccines and reconcile with the Stock Cards
        to determine discrepancies, if any.


   D. Require the MHO to reconcile with the PHO the number of vaccine  doses
      received for the five brands of vaccine, namely, Sinovac, Astrazeneca,
      Pfizer, Janssen, Moderna;

   E. Require the MHO to report the accurate number of vaccines on  hand  as
      of                 December 31, 2021.

We recommended that management agreed to:


   1. Conduct close monitoring of the PPAs by the implementing department to
      speed up the implementation of four PPAs totaling ₱288,000.00  and  to
      enhance monitoring activities on the implementation of PPAs funded  by
      the national government; and


   2. Direct the Municipal Accountant to conduct  regular  verification  and
      reconciliation  of  unexpended   balances   of   fund   transfer   and
      return/remit balances to source agency
Instruct the Property Custodian to coordinate with the Municipal Accountant on the proper and correct classification, as well as complete recording of the PPE accounts;
Investigate and clear all discrepancies between the physical count and the accounting records and make the necessary reclassifications/corrections; and

Require the Municipal Accountant to prepare monthly the Bank Reconciliation Statements for all bank accounts and submit to the Office of the Auditor regularly, in compliance with Sections 3.1, 3.2 and 3.3 of COA Circular No. 96-011, to ensure that all bank debit/credit memos are immediately discovered and recorded in the books;

Direct the Municipal Treasurer to properly record entries upon receipt of funds and issuance of checks per transaction date and cause the updating of cashbooks for all bank accounts based on the actual occurrence of transactions;
Direct the Municipal Accountant to promptly secure bank statements or snapshots regularly from the bank on a monthly basis to ensure the timely preparation of the bank reconciliation statements;

Direct the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer to reconcile their records regularly to ensure the accuracy and correctness of the Cash in Bank accounts reconciled balances reflected in the financial statements.
We recommended and the Management agreed that the DO’s cash advances be based  on the net amount of payroll and subsequently, liquidate or refund the excess  amount five days after each pay period.
observations.managementAction: The DO made cash advances  for some payrolls in excess of the total pay  period.

observations.recommendation: Direct the Municipal Accountant to throughly verify each disbursement as to the completeness and propriety of the supporting documents to strengthen internal control in the disbursement process.

Require the Municipal Accountant to stop the practice of recording to direct expense the purchases of supplies and materials for stock, consumption or distribution and to record it as expense only upon distribution and utilization;
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant already directed her accounting staff to take up as inventories procurement of supplies and materials.

observations.recommendation: Require the Municipal Accountant to maintain the required perpetual inventory records, pursuant to Section 114 of the New Government Accounting System (NGAS) for Local Government Units (LGUs), Volume I;
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant instructed his accounting staff to observe the perpetual inventory system in the recording of procurements of supplies and materials.

observations.recommendation: Require the Property Custodian to immediately prepare and submit to the Municipal Accountant the Summary of Supplies and Materials Issued (SSMI) to be supported with the RIS.

In view of the foregoing, we recommended that Management:
a. Require the Municipal Accountant to reconcile the accounting records with the report
of the Inventory Committee;
b. Instruct the Inventory Committee to coordinate with the Municipal Accountant on the
proper and correct classification of the PPE accounts; and
c. Investigate and clear all discrepancies between the physical count and the accounting
records and make the necessary reclassifications/corrections.

AOM No. 2020-005 dated February 10, 2020 was issued to inform the Management of
the deficiencies noted.

In view of the foregoing, we recommended that:
a. The DO base her cash advance on the net amount of payroll and liquidate or refund
the excess amount, if any, after the payment of salaries, wages, allowances and
similar disbursements 5 days after each pay period; and
b. The Payroll Clerks prepare the payroll on a timely basis to support claim for cash
advances.

AOM No. 2019-008 dated September 18, 2019 was issued to inform the Management of
the deficiencies noted.

In view of the foregoing, we recommended the following:
a. That officials and employees concerned liquidate immediately the cash advances
granted them or refund the amount if the same could not be liquidated;
b. That demand letters be sent to the concerned officials and employees and if remained
unheeded, the Local Chief Executive may cause or order the suspension of payment
of their salaries until they have fully liquidated thereof; and
c. Instruct the Municipal Accountant to monitor the liquidation of cash advances to
ensure that they are liquidated within the reglementary period or as soon as the
purpose for which it was given has been served.

AOM No. 2020-001 dated January 28, 2020 was issued to inform the Management of the
deficiencies noted.

We recommended that Management fast track the gathering of necessary documents for
the opening of individual ATM savings accounts in order that necessary training for the
personnel in-charged be started and for safe payment of salaries to employees thru ATM,
in compliance to Sections 2 and 4(8) of PD No. 1445. Likewise, the reports being
prepared and maintained by the DO will be minimized and to avoid the risk of handling
huge amount of cash and exposing them to possible loss through theft.

AOM No. 2020-001 dated January 28, 2020 was issued to inform the Management of the
deficiencies noted.

We recommended that the Management:
a. Direct the MT to impose the immediate payment of unpaid monthly rentals and to
strictly enforce effective collection scheme. Likewise, ensure that payment of rentals
are updated before the renewal of the contract is made;
b. Direct the Municipal Accountant to take-up uncollected rentals as receivable; and
c. Direct the MT to monitor strictly the implementation of the terms and conditions of the
Contract especially in the prompt payment of rent and the imposition of the sanction
embodied thereto.

AOM No. 2020-003 dated January 28, 2020 was issued to inform the Management of the
deficiencies noted.

In view of the following, we recommended that Management:
a. Direct the personnel concerned to institute collection procedure and demand from the
subject former OIC-MT the settlement of the said receivable accounts; and
b. Instruct the Municipal Accountant to exert extra effort to determine the details of the
“Accounts Receivable” and “Loans Receivable – Others” and make the necessary
actions to request for write-off, if such details are impossible to establish, in
accordance with COA Circular No. 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016.

AOM No. 2020-004 dated January 28, 2028 was issued to inform the Management of the
deficiencies noted.
a. That officials and employees concerned liquidate immediately the cash advances granted them or refund the amount if the same could not be liquidated;

b. That demand letters be sent to the concerned officials and employees and if remained unheeded, the Local Chief Executive may cause or order the suspension of payment of their salaries until they have fully liquidated thereof;

c. Instruct the Municipal Accountant to monitor the liquidation of cash advances to ensure that they are liquidated within the reglementary period or as soon as the purpose for which it was given has been served; and
d. Refrain from granting additional cash advances to officials and employees who failed to liquidate their previous cash advances.

a. Assign a specific personnel who would take charge of the monitoring and submission of contracts, purchase orders and all supporting documents forming part thereof to the Auditor’s Office, within five (5) working days from the execution of contracts and issuance of purchase orders pursuant to COA Circular No. 2009-01 dated February 12, 2009; and
b. Cause the attendance of the responsible personnel to seminars and trainings related to their job in order to enrich their knowledge on the tasks assigned to them.

We recommended that Management follow-up the previous arrangement with the government bank for the opening of individual ATM savings accounts as a means of improving fiscal management in accordance with Sections 2 and 4(8) of PD No. 1445, to minimize the reports being prepared and maintained by the DO and to avoid the risk of handling huge amount of cash and exposing them to possible loss through theft.

We recommended that Management direct the concerned LDRRMO to strictly comply with Item 5.1.5 of COA Circular No. 2012-002 on the submission of the Monthly Reports on the Sources and Utilization of the LDRRMF so that COA verification and evaluation of the entries thereon can be performed on a timely basis.
a. Direct the Municipal Treasurer (MT) to process the execution of Market Stall Contracts Lease;

d. Direct the MT to monitor strictly the implementation of the terms and conditions of the Contract especially in the prompt payment of rent and the imposition of the sanction embodied thereto.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant exert effort to reconcile the balances of the two reciprocal accounts and make the necessary adjustments thereto, based on the results of the reconciliation.

We recommended that Management direct the concerned LDRRMO to submit the Monthly Reports on the Sources and Utilization of the LDRRMF in accordance Section 5.1.5 of COA Circular No. 2012-002 using the attached format in Annex Band duly certified correct by the Municipal Accountant to ensure its correctness.

The Municipal Accountant was instructed during the exit conference that unexpended        balances of these fund transfers must be returned to the Source Agency after the        completion of the projects.

Direct the Municipal Accountant to effect immediately the transfer of the unexpended balances of the QRF and DRRM-MOOE of the previous years, as well as for the current year to the Special Trust Fund under the account “Trust Liability-DRRM” in the Trust Fund books; and

Direct the Municipal Treasurer to transfer the corresponding amount from the General Fund account to the Trust Fund account.

We recommended that Management submit to the Audit Team a copy of all contracts, purchase orders, and the like, and all supporting documents forming part thereof, within five (5) working days from the execution of the contracts and issuance of purchase orders pursuant to COA Circular No. 2009-01 dated February 12, 2009.

We recommended that Management submit to the Audit Team a copy of all contracts, purchase orders, and the like, and all supporting documents forming part thereof, within five (5) working days from the execution of the contracts and issuance of purchase orders pursuant to COA Circular No. 2009-01 dated February 12, 2009.

We recommended that Management follow-up the previous arrangement with the government bank for the opening of individual ATM savings accounts as a means of improving fiscal management in accordance with Sections 2 and 4(8) of PD No. 1445, to minimize the reports being prepared and maintained by the DO and to avoid the risk of handling huge amount of cash and exposing them to possible loss through theft.

We recommended that Management direct the concerned LDRRMO through the LDRRMC and LDC to submit the Monthly Reports on the Sources and Utilization of the LDRRMF certified correct by the Municipal Accountant within the time prescribed under COA Circular No. 2012-002 dated September 12, 2012.

In view of the foregoing, we recommended that Management:
 a. Require the Inventory Committee to conduct a complete physical count of all PPEs
owned by the Municipality and submit to the Audit Team the Report on the Physical
Count of Property, Plant and Equipment (RPCPPE);
 b. Require the Municipal Accountant and Supply Officer to reconcile their records; and
 c. Investigate and clear all discrepancies between the physical count and the
accounting records and make the necessary adjustments and corrections.

We recommended that Management:
 a. Direct the Municipal Accountant to effect immediately the transfer of the
unexpended balances of the QRF and DRRM-MOOE of the previous years, as well
as for the current year to the Special Trust Fund under the account “Trust Liability-
DRRM” in the Trust Fund books; and
 b. Direct the Municipal Treasurer to transfer the corresponding amount from the
General Fund account to the Trust Fund account.

We recommended that Management direct the concerned LDRRMO to submit the
Monthly Reports on the Sources and Utilization of the LDRRMF in accordance Section
5.1.5 of COA Circular No. 2012-002 using the attached format in Annex B and duly
certified correct by the Municipal Accountant to ensure its correctness.

In view of the foregoing, we recommended the following:
 a) That officials and employees concerned liquidate immediately the cash advances
granted them or refund the amount if the same could not be liquidated;
 b) That demand letters be sent to the concerned officials and employees and if
remained unheeded, the Local Chief Executive may cause or order the suspension
of payment of their salaries until they have fully liquidated thereof;
 c) Instruct the Municipal Accountant to monitor the liquidation of cash advances to
ensure that they are liquidated within the reglementary period or as soon as the
purpose for which it was given has been served; and
 d) Refrain from granting additional cash advances to officials and employees who failed
to liquidate their previous cash advances.

We recommended that Management:
 d. Impose strict compliance with existing ordinances on proper waste disposal to
minimize accumulation of residual wastes;
 e. Coordinate with other agencies or entities, government or private, which could
provide assistance in the disposal of these residual wastes; and
 f. Exert extra effort to adopt measures on how to transfer these wastes to a long term
storage or disposal facility or sanitary landfill, in compliance with Section 32 of RA
9003.

We recommended that Management:
 a) Implement promptly projects that were funded out of fund transfers;
 b) Follow-up for the approval of the required DED and the submission of other
requirements to expedite the implementation of the project; and
 c) Assign personnel to specifically prepare and submit the required documents to avoid
similar problem in the future.

We recommended that Management:
 a. Implement projects that were funded out of fund transfers within the prescribed
timelines, in accordance with the guidelines provided in DILG-DBM JMC No. 2017-3
dated May 23, 2017 and the contracts, to avoid its consequences;
 b. Closely monitor the implementation of the projects and perform necessary actions to
be taken in case of delay such as imposing liquidated damages, if warranted; and
 c. Henceforth, furnish the Audit Team copy of the contracts within 5 days after their
perfection and other reports needed in the evaluation of the project implementation.

We recommended that Management submit to the Audit Team a copy of all contracts,
purchase orders, and the like, and all supporting documents forming part thereof, within
five (5) working days from the execution of the contracts and issuance of purchase
orders pursuant to COA Circular No. 2009-01 dated February 12, 2009.

We recommended that Management follow-up the previous arrangement with the
government bank for the opening of individual ATM savings accounts as a means of
improving fiscal management in accordance with Sections 2 and 4(8) of PD No. 1445, to
minimize the reports being prepared and maintained by the DO and to avoid the risk of
handling huge amount of cash and exposing them to possible loss through theft.

We recommended that Management indicate all the necessary data in the Purchase Orders (POs) to easily determine the compliance by the suppliers’/contractors’ of their responsibilities on the contract, hence, penalties and correction of deficiencies could be imposed immediately and wastage of the limited funds of the municipality could be avoided.
We recommended that Management prepare the APP in accordance with the format prescribed in GPPB Resolution No. 20-2015and Section 7 of the IRR of RA No. 9184.
We recommended that Management return the unexpended balance of completed projects to the NGA concerned to finance other developmental projects.

We recommended that at the end of each business day, the Collecting Officers be required to accomplish the Report of Collections and Deposits (RCD), submit it to the Revenue Liquidating Officer, together with the duplicate copies of all official receipts issued and turn over all cash collected.

We recommended that the Liquidating Officer be required to turn-over all remittances received to the ICO-Municipal Treasurer daily. Likewise, we also recommended that the ICO-Municipal Treasurer deposit intact all remitted amounts with the authorized depository bank in accordance with Section 32 of the Manual on the NGAS for LGUs, Volume I.

We recommended that the accountable officer update and balance daily her cashbooks to establish cash accountability at the end of the month and observe diligently the ruling and footing of the cashbooks to avoid various errors in the posting of transactions. Henceforth, comply strictly Section 181 (b) & (c) of Government Accounting and Auditing Manual, Volume I.
We recommended that the DO and the Municipal Accountant reconcile their cashbook and subsidiary ledger balances at least quarterly to adjust the reconciling items in their respective books/records.

We recommended that Management require the Municipal Accountant to submit the required supporting documents, to observe proper accounting and recording of transactions, to make proper reclassification of accounts and to monitor and review liquidations to ensure that funds were spent in accordance with the intended purpose.

a. Require the person in charge to secure a copy of the MOA and project proposal/plan and submit them to the Audit Team; and
b. Require the Municipal Accountant to ensure the completeness of supporting documents prior to the approval of the disbursement vouchers, to avoid audit suspensions.

We recommended that Management revisit the manpower complement of the Office of the Municipal Engineer and adopt measures to resolve the inadequacy in the office. Likewise, we recommend the designation of an engineer to supervise and monitor the implementation of the Municipality’s infrastructure projects to avoid loss or wastage of the resources of the Municipality.

We recommended that Management submit to the Audit Team a copy of all contracts, purchase orders, and the like, and all supporting documents forming part thereof by reference or incorporation within five (5) working days from the execution of the contracts and issuance of purchase orders pursuant to COA Circular No. 2009-001 dated February 12, 2009 so that review, inspection and evaluation could be made accordingly.

We recommended that Management:

a. Require the Inventory Committee to conduct a complete physical count of all PPEs
owned by the Municipality and submit to the Audit Team the Report on the Physical
Count of Property, Plant and Equipment (RPCPPE);

b. Require the Municipal Accountant to reconcile the accounting records with the report of
the Inventory Committee;

c. Instruct the Inventory Committee to coordinate with the Municipal Accountant on the
proper and correct classification of the PPE accounts; and

d. Investigate and clear all discrepancies between the physical count and the accounting
records and make the necessary reclassifications/corrections.

We recommended that Management:

a. Require the person in charge to prepare and submit lease contract, equipment
utilization report and proof of services rendered by the rented equipment, every time the
Municipality resort to equipment rental;

b. Refrain from resorting to equipment rental when the equipment owned by the
Municipality are available;

c. Submit justification as to the reason why the Municipality resorted to equipment
rental when they have their own heavy equipment and why contract for equipment
rental when the project was already completed; and

d. Require the Municipal Accountant to ensure the completeness of supporting
documents prior to the approval of the disbursement vouchers, to avoid audit
suspensions.

We recommended that Management:

a. Require the person in charge to comply with the prescribed bidding process, as well as
the documentary requirements as required under the 2016 Revised IRR of RA No.
9184;

b. Refund the overpayment for oil and lubricants in the amount of ₱36,496.00;

c. Henceforth, require the personnel in charge of the implementation of the projects to
closely monitor the utilization of the construction materials and other supplies used for
the completion of such projects; and

d. Require the Municipal Accountant to ensure the completeness of supporting documents
prior to the approval of the disbursement vouchers, to avoid audit suspensions and
disallowances.

We recommended that Management:

a. Require the persons concerned to prepare and submit the required documents and
other requirements necessary for the repair of motor vehicles and other equipment;

b. Require the Municipal Accountant to ensure the completeness of supporting
documents prior to the approval of the disbursement vouchers, to avoid audit
suspensions; and

c. Require the Municipal Accountant to check and examine each supporting documents
to ensure they were properly filled up, so that review and evaluation could be made
accordingly.

We recommended that Management:

a. Require the persons concerned to prepare and submit the required documents and
other requirements relative to the purchase of gasoline, oil and lubricants;

b. Require the Municipal Accountant to ensure the completeness of supporting
documents prior to the approval of the disbursement vouchers, to avoid audit
suspensions; and

c. Henceforth, strictly adhere to the provisions of the Manual on Audit for Fuel
Consumption of Government Motor Vehicles prescribed in COA Circular No. 77-61
dated September 26, 1977, to effectively control the gasoline, oil and lubricant
expenditures.

We recommended that Management indicate all the necessary data in the Purchase Orders
(POs) to easily determine the compliance by the suppliers’/contractors’ of their
responsibilities on the contract, hence, penalties and correction of deficiencies could be
imposed immediately and wastage of the limited funds of the municipality could be avoided.

We recommended that Management submit to the Audit Team a copy of all contracts,
purchase orders, and the like, and all supporting documents forming part thereof, within
five (5) working days from the execution of the contracts and issuance of purchase
orders pursuant to COA Circular No. 2009-01 dated February 12, 2009.

We recommended that Management prepare the APP in accordance with the format
prescribed in GPPB Resolution No. 20-2015and Section 7 of the IRR of RA No. 9184.

We recommended that Management return the unexpended balance of completed
projects to the NGA concerned to finance other developmental projects.

We recommended that Management:

a. Require the Collecting Officers concerned to accomplish the RCD at the end of each
business day and submit it to the Revenue Liquidating Officer, together with the
duplicate copies of all official receipts issued and turn over all cash collected;

b. Direct the ICO-Municipal Treasurer to monitor the status of remittances and ensure that
all collecting officers remit all their collections daily and intact; and

c. Require the Municipal Accountant to review reports submitted by the ICO-Municipal
Treasurer to ensure that all collections were recorded and taken up on the month/year
they were earned and make the necessary adjustments on the affected Income and
Equity accounts.

We recommended that at the end of each business day, the Collecting Officers be required
to accomplish the Report of Collections and Deposits (RCD), submit it to the Revenue
Liquidating Officer, together with the duplicate copies of all official receipts issued and turn
over all cash collected.

We recommended that the Liquidating Officer be required to turn-over all remittances
received to the ICO-Municipal Treasurer daily. Likewise, we also recommended that the
ICO-Municipal Treasurer deposit intact all remitted amounts with the authorized depository
bank in accordance with Section 32 of the Manual on the NGAS for LGUs, Volume I.

We recommended that Management:

a. Direct the Municipal Accountant to prepare the Bank Reconciliation Statements on a
monthly basis to immediately determine errors and other reconciling items;

b. Require the Municipal Accountant and the ICO-Municipal Treasurer to regularly
reconcile their records and books and make necessary adjustments/ corrections, if
warranted; and

c. Submit to the Auditor the Bank Reconciliation Statements (BRS) including the paid
checks and original copies of debit/credit memos.

We recommended that the accountable officer update and balance daily her cashbooks
to establish cash accountability at the end of the month and observe diligently the ruling
and footing of the cashbooks to avoid various errors in the posting of transactions.
Henceforth, comply strictly Section 181 (b) & (c) of Government Accounting and Auditing
Manual, Volume I.

We recommended that the DO and the Municipal Accountant reconcile their cashbook
and subsidiary ledger balances at least quarterly to adjust the reconciling items in their
respective books/records.

We recommended that Management follow-up the previous arrangement with the
government bank for the opening of individual ATM savings accounts as a means of
improving fiscal management in accordance with Sections 2 and 4(8) of PD No. 1445, to
minimize the reports being prepared and maintained by the DO and to avoid the risk of
handling huge amount of cash and exposing them to possible loss through theft.

We recommended that Management require the Municipal Accountant to submit the
required supporting documents, to observe proper accounting and recording of
transactions, to make proper reclassification of accounts and to monitor and review
liquidations to ensure that funds were spent in accordance with the intended purpose.

We recommended that Management:

c. Require the person in charge to secure a copy of the MOA and project proposal/plan
and submit them to the Audit Team; and

d. Require the Municipal Accountant to ensure the completeness of supporting documents
prior to the approval of the disbursement vouchers, to avoid audit suspensions.

We recommended that Management direct the concerned LDRRMO through the LDRRMC
and LDC to submit the Monthly Reports on the Sources and Utilization of the LDRRMF
certified correct by the Municipal Accountant within the time prescribed under COA Circular
No. 2012-002 dated September 12, 2012.

We recommended that Management revisit the manpower complement of the Office of the
Municipal Engineer and adopt measures to resolve the inadequacy in the office.

Likewise, we recommend the designation of an engineer to supervise and monitor the
implementation of the Municipality’s infrastructure projects to avoid loss or wastage of

a. Stop utilizing SEF for purposes that are not in accordance with Sec.100(c) and DECS-DBM-DILG Joint Circular (JC) No. 01, series of 1998 dated April 14, 1998, prescribing the rules and regulations in the utilization of SEF; b. Prioritize programs and activities in line with the purpose for which the Fund was created.

a. Sections 39 and 107 of Presidential Decree 1445 requiring all accountable officers to render monthly reports of their transactions in time and mode as set by law; and b. COA Circular No. 95-006 dated May 18, 1995, requiring accountable officer to turn-over documents within the prescribed period.

We recommended that the Municipality comply strictly with Section 2.2 and Sub-sections 2.2.1 and 2.2.3 of COA Circular 2013-004 dated January 30, 2013, to ensure that the public is informed of any subsequent projects that will be implemented to foster transparency and accountability and also to encourage public participation.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant to effect adjustments on deductions and to inform the employee concerned to make proper arrangements with the private lending institutions on repayments so as not to reduce the net take home pay as prescribed under the General Provisions of the GAA of FY 2015. We recommended that Municipal Accountant should be prudent in issuing the Certificate of Net Take Home Pay to their employees, which is one of the requisites when applying for a loan, to avoid availment of loans beyond their paying capacity.

a. Initiate the payment of employees’ salaries, allowances, fringe benefits and other emoluments through the bank, as a means of improving fiscal management in accordance with Sections 2 and 4(8) of PD 1445, with the expected result of minimizing the attendant risks identified with cash-paid payrolls and easing the effects of manpower attrition; b. Make a representation/arrange with a government bank for the opening of individual ATM savings accounts by the employees through a Memorandum of Agreement (MOA); and c. Adopt the payroll-thru-bank scheme upon approval of the MOA.
